items.q: Were medieval serfs the same as slaves?
items.correct: No - serfs were unfree and tied to the land, but they held customary rights, worked their own plots, and were not generally bought and sold as individual property.
items.misconception: Serfs were slaves with no rights of any kind.
items.random_wrong: Serfs were paid professional soldiers of the lord.
items.why: Collapsing serfdom into slavery erases the legal and customary structures that defined manorial society.

items.q: Was the medieval economy static and unchanging?
items.correct: No - between about 1000 and 1300 population grew strongly, new towns were founded, field rotation improved, and trade fairs and banking expanded.
items.misconception: Nothing changed economically during the so-called Dark Ages.
items.random_wrong: Medieval Europe had no money and used only barter.
items.why: The 'Dark Ages' label hides the commercial revolution of the High Middle Ages, which this topic is largely about.

items.q: Did the Black Death affect only Europe?
items.correct: No - the pandemic spread along trade routes across Asia, the Middle East and North Africa, devastating cities such as Cairo as well as European ones.
items.misconception: The plague of the fourteenth century was a purely European event.
items.random_wrong: The Black Death was confined to the British Isles.
items.why: World history demands the trans-regional view: the same trade networks that built prosperity carried the disease.

items.q: In the feudal system, what did a vassal typically receive in return for service?
items.correct: A fief - land granted by a lord
items.random_wrong: A cash salary paid by the king's bank
items.why: It's A fief - land granted by a lord. “A guild membership” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: An association of artisans in one trade that regulated training, quality and prices
items.misconception: A tax collected by the Church
items.why: Correct answer: An association of artisans in one trade that regulated training, quality and prices. “A tax collected by the Church” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How did the Black Death affect labour in Western Europe?
items.correct: Labour shortages pushed wages up and helped weaken serfdom
items.misconception: Wages collapsed because there were too many workers
items.random_wrong: It had no measurable economic effects
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Labour shortages pushed wages up and helped weaken serfdom. “Wages collapsed because there were too many workers” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Overland and sea routes linking China, Central Asia, the Middle East and Europe, carrying goods and ideas
items.why: Correct answer: Overland and sea routes linking China, Central Asia, the Middle East and Europe, carrying goods and ideas. “Roman military roads across Gaul” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Black Death reach Europe via Sicily?
items.why: The real figure is 1347 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
